explanations.1: This special line tells the system to use bash to run this script, no matter which shell the user is currently using.
explanations.4: The 'local -r' keywords make this variable local to the function and read-only (can't be changed), and the '${1:-}' syntax means 'use the first argument, or an empty string if no argument was given'.
explanations.7: The '[[ -z ... ]]' test checks if the variable is empty, and '&&' means 'if that's true, then run the echo command'; the '>&2' redirects the message to the error stream instead of normal output.
explanations.18: The 'command -v' is a safe way to check if a program exists, the '|| true' means 'if command -v fails, pretend it succeeded anyway', and the '$(...)' wraps all that to capture what it prints.
